title: Evangelion It Is Not Perfect
wikitext:
[]: Evangelion: It Is (Not) Perfect Post by Eva Yojimbo » Sat Apr 24, 2010 1:41 am Essentially what I'm wondering is this: Does anyone really care that NGE isn't perfect? Would anyone really want it to be? What would perfecting really do to add to the impact NGE has already had both within its medium and with numerous individuals and elsewhere? Why are we, or, more importantly, why should we be obsessed with something's lack of perfection? This subject has come up a lot recently. There's a discussion starting HERE about it in which I say: I wrote: ...NGE would be a much lesser series if it lacked its so-called flaws because it's precisely those flaws that allows to be so amazingly dynamic, so rich, to touch on so many things. When it's at its most unrestrained is when it's at its most overwhelmingly powerful, and that power would be diluted if it was so perfectly written and controlled. It needs that sense of chaos and things breaking down to achieve the visceral impact it does. If you try to rein it in and perfect it I think you remove a great deal of that.

[]: Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Sat Apr 24, 2010 4:03 am "Imperfections"? Show me something absolutely "perfect", then we'll start talking about slight "imperfections". The word "imperfections" is just silly anyway. It's a way of saying something is flawed without any real dis-tasteful consequence. If something is flawed, say so. Things that don't go perfectly simply force the creative mind to find a way around it, without compromising much of the art. Sometimes people notice this, sometimes they don't. But because EVERYTHING has examples of art-gone-wrong, causing things to stray from the pie-in-the-sky concept of "perfection", then there's no real definitive example of "perfection". So nothing can be "imperfect" either. It just is what it is. Anyone who knows anything about the production of The Fugitive knows exactly what I'm talking about. The real question is: "Is this art flawed in a distracting way?" As for NGE, there are examples of rush jobs, animation out-sourcing, and even some confusing arcs labeled as plot-holes. But I don't find these things distracting from the series. So I wouldn't say that they bother me. (Unless I see the umpteenth "Why does Kaworu..." thread pop up.) The budgetary and time restrictions placed upon NGE helped make it what it is. If they had a larger budget, I'm not sure we'd even see Asuka's mind-rape scene in episode 22 played out in the same way. And that's if we'd see it at all. Money talks, and it says: "More Eva fights!" Asuka's battle might have played out in physical combat that mentally effected her, or something. I mean, it did the same with Shinji when he was bashing Eva-03 in episode 18. As the second half of the series progresses, I can see the budget getting smaller and smaller. GAINAX will create a whole episode around just the human characters (episode 15, I believe) to hold to put funds away for one episode with a physical Eva fight later down the road. And no, that zebra-striped sphere Angel didn't count as an actual fight. Shinji just got stuck for a while, then broke out at the end. Not as expensive animation as what was seen in the first half of the series or the animation in episode 18 and 19. And yet it was all of the ways Anno used his budget-saving animation techniques that really made Neon Genesis Evangelion that thematically heavy animated show from Japan that happens to have mecha-esque elements in it. This is one of the reasons I'm very interested in the decisions Anno is making for Rebuild. With a budget that's getting BIGGER with each movie (the complete OPPOSITE from the show's circumstance) will Anno still maintain the thematic and philosophical elements that helped distinguish the original TV series from the other "giant robot shows" among the big-budget visuals? Or is he just gonna sacrifice ALL of it for clean animation? (I haven't seen 2.0 yet, so don't tell me.)

[]: Post by Reichu » Sat Apr 24, 2010 8:14 am Jimbo: I have absolutely no qualms over NGE not being perfect. I've also been in enough "NGE isn't perfect" threads that they should have given me cancer by now. My rant against your "bubbly froth" has less to do with NGE specifically and more to do with frustration. Every time someone mentions the possibility that something might be due to e.g. bad writing, you show up and say, "No, no, it's not bad writing, Anno is such a genius that any potential flaw only makes NGE better!" I don't know about you, but this sort of thing does put certain limits on the possibilities we can consider... You seem to treat it like a very bad thing that a flaw might impact NGE in a negative way, and go to great lengths to put a "positive spin" on everything. Also note that since I was only capable of such ridiculously unconditional idolatry when I was manic, that cued my possibly unnecessary comment about you being high. In any event, it would be nice if we could talk about the show without having to constantly hear rhetoric that is practically religious in its zeal. Anno isn't a god. The series didn't even come totally from his head, horrible an idea as that might be to consider. And since you're apparently willing to contemplate the notion that NGE might have flaws, perhaps you could be a little more willing to discuss these flaws as actual, well, FLAWS -- and I'm not talking about cute little moles -- instead of just "talking the talk". This "perfection" stuff seems very much beside the point. Well, from the perspective of what I was saying, anyway. I dunno about those other guys.

[]: Post by Merridian » Sat Apr 24, 2010 9:34 am Jimbo wrote: If I were to think of counterparts to the above works I might mention Paradise Lost, Madame Bovary, and 2001: ASO.

[]: Post by Joseph the PRPD » Sat Apr 24, 2010 9:56 am Nothing is perfect. Eva has it's flaws and I'm not bothered by it. So yeah, I don't care.

[]: Post by tomrule123 » Sat Apr 24, 2010 11:01 am Have I ever encountered a perfect movie, picture or event? No. NGE had budget limits, a confusing plot and two endings that still didn't satisfy everyone. (and probably a bit too much fan service... not good) Even Rebuild of EVA is not perfect (shorter storyline and lack of character development). We can't change history, so we have to go along with an imperfect NGE. But hey, even these flaws didn't decrease popularity and that is the important thing about it.

[]: Post by J_Faulkner » Sat Apr 24, 2010 12:06 pm Let me speak to people in general here, including those that are reading this thread (i.e. not to any specific person, as I have done in that quote of mine in this thread): the issue is not whether Evangelion is perfect or not. We generally agree it's not perfect (see http://forum.evageeks.org/viewtopic.php?t=8469&start=0). No piece of animation is perfect. That much is obvious. The issue is acknowledging Evangelion's flaws when someone brings them up without the instant reaction of trying to defend Evangelion by saying the flaws don't seriously detract from the show. One doesn't erase the flaws by doing this, merely shift attention away from it. Also, what is considered a detraction varies from person to person. It is in part subjective. What doesn't affect you can very well affect someone else, because we all have different personalities, which are partly based on genetics. This is a basic psychological fact that cannot be denied. Unless someone would like to deny people's genetic make-ups. So the point is that if one is distracted by a flaw in Evangelion to a significant degree, and this is actually based on a fact, then you cannot just say that it is an insignificant flaw, and magic it away. It is easy then to see that continuing to argue that such a flaw doesn't matter doesn't make any sense whatsoever, and to do so simply trivializes other people's legitimate interpretations and denies their perspectives, using dogmatic reasoning that tries to sound objective but is ultimately grounded in subjectivity. Let's have some examples here. Some people did not like the series ending partly because it is not well animated (e.g. ProfessorKaos64, http://forum.evageeks.org/viewtopic.php?t=8914). This is a fact – it was not well animated. These people may be distracted by this because of a like for more animation. This could be tied to viewing history and/or his subjective personal preferences. Note that this could be the case even if this person has been exposed to intellectual art before. Note that this does not mean that the person cannot still enjoy the ending overall. So to argue that the poor animation in the series ending does not detract significantly from the experience of Evangelion for everyone , you'd have to say there's something wrong with this person's knowledge, experience of art and his personal preferences. In other words, ad hom nonsense. Some people find that some aspects of the show are geared towards selling merchandise, and find this distracting (e.g. Senor Squiid – note that he found Evangelion to be "incredibly" imperfect, which shows again that his beef is not that Evangelion is merely imperfect, but to an extent that affected him to a significant degree). This is a fact – e.g. obviously, Rei's physical appearance was designed partly with marketing considerations in mind. These people may be distracted by this because they feel that with less marketing considerations (not just the simple-minded straw-man either/or no marketing considerations), there would be a stronger focus on just the storyline and the message the creators wanted to portray. You cannot argue against this by saying that most other animes do this, because 2+ wrongs don't make a right – the fact that there are marketing considerations in Evangelion remains and causes a distraction. Some people find that the level of fan-service in Evangelion is distracting (e.g. psychosis090, http://forum.evageeks.org/viewtopic.php?t=8290&postdays=0&postorder=asc&start=120). It is a fact that there is fan-service. These people may be distracted by the fan-service as they feel that less fan-service (not just the simple-minded straw-man either/or no fan-service), there would be a stronger focus on the storyline and the message the creators wanted to portray. You cannot argue against this by saying that most other animes do this, because 2+ wrongs don't make a right – the fact that there is fan-service in Evangelion remains and causes a distraction. You cannot argue against this by saying that Anno intended to put all this in just to subvert it later on, because from a neutral perspective, this is unclear (see http://forum.evageeks.org/viewtopic.php?t=8290&postdays=0&postorder=asc&start=0 and http://forum.evageeks.org/viewtopic.php?t=8469&start=0). So if you include e.g. more animation in the final episodes in the series, less marketing considerations and less fan-service, then you can get a product that is a significant improvement for at least some people. I'm sure people here can come up with more examples of their own. To argue against this, what I consider a very basic truth, you'd have resort to attacking the people that hold the viewpoints and not the reasoning in these viewpoints, because as shown above, you cannot do this. You'd have to go on and on about lack of art experience, ignorance about art history, personality defects, being uncultured, unintelligent, lacking critical thinking, lacking understanding, lack of awareness that viewpoints have been objectively proven to be false (scientifically), people being thick etc. etc. In other words, ad hom bullshit that adds up to nothing other than a logical fallacy. (Either this or try and hide these criticisms by instantly lavishing praise on some other aspects or saying that "hey, most things do this, so it must be OK! I have totally no idea why you'd hold [this or that] against Evangelion; something must be wrong with you.") You only have to see the fan-service thread to see this in action, against not only myself but psychosis090 (God forbid anyone would actually agree with me based on my arguments), and in the threads with SenorSquiid ( http://forum.evageeks.org/viewtopic.php?t=8977 and http://forum.evageeks.org/viewtopic.php?t=8759&start=60). Now, I'm fine with receiving such behavior, since I have a large armoury of tactics that I can use in reply, but when this happens to new members like psychosis090 and SenorSquiid, who are probably intimidated into silence, that's when I feel compelled to raise the issue up for greater discussion. What I find astonishing is that this kind of attitude extends to people that are fans of the show! People that really like the show. ProfessorKaos64, in the linked thread, says that "end of eva … ruled" and SenorSquiid says in the quote in this thread that he "loves" Evangelion "to death" and that it's "important to [him] in a special way." We're not talking about haters that trash Evangelion, but people that appreciate it, even love it. So what exactly is the problem in accepting criticisms of Evangelion which fans cite as significantly distracting from their experience of it, when there is no logical reason to deny these criticisms? Why can't we as a community accept this and move on? Reichu raised a great point in the other thread which alludes to the two-way nature of engagement. If we continue to deny legitimate criticisms, we are not engaging with but denying other fans who criticize Evangelion in any significant way. We are shutting up shop to people that can increase the richness and diversity of Evangelion fandom, keeping it a closed circle for people that cannot or will not contemplate that it is entirely logical to dislike certain aspects of Evangelion but still like the show overall on balance. Do we want diversity or do we just want to make others think the same way we do, regardless of what happens? This is the choice I feel we are making here, and this is the question I pose to everyone.

[]: Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Sat Apr 24, 2010 12:44 pm Merridian wrote: 2001:ASO is not perfect. The stargate scene blows and there aren%u2019t enough people running away from explosions.

[]: Post by ran1 » Sat Apr 24, 2010 1:10 pm Shit. Jimbo and Merri beat me to what I was going to say In any event, just allow me to +1 what you guys said with the exception of Hamlet statement. Hate to gripe, but I prefer King Lear, Macbeth and Midsummer's Night Dream

[]: Post by AuraTwilight » Sat Apr 24, 2010 1:16 pm Perfect stuff is boring, anyway.

[]: Post by J_Faulkner » Sat Apr 24, 2010 1:22 pm AuraTwilight wrote: Perfect stuff is boring, anyway.

[]: Post by Merridian » Sat Apr 24, 2010 1:26 pm J_Faulkner wrote: The issue is acknowledging Evangelion's flaws when someone brings them up without the instant reaction of trying to defend Evangelion by saying the flaws don't seriously detract from the show.

[]: Post by J_Faulkner » Sat Apr 24, 2010 1:35 pm Merridian wrote: Saying that his directing isn't good enough is pretty much the same as saying “I want it to have more animation”; the audience has no right to say that to someone else’s work—if the audience wants more animation that badly, they should go do their OWN work and appeal to their own senses of entertainment.

[]: Post by CyberXIII » Sat Apr 24, 2010 1:45 pm There's no such thing as a perfect anime. Everything has flaws, and through those flaws do we have better anime. Besides, if we had a perfect anime, no one would recognize it as such because anime fans will nitpick it to death.

[]: Post by Merridian » Sat Apr 24, 2010 1:47 pm JF wrote: I can't criticize the hamburger I bought because I thought it tasted like shit, because I didn't make it and the owner's profits were low so he replaced the meat with dog shit?

[]: Post by Reichu » Sat Apr 24, 2010 1:48 pm J_Faulkner wrote: Second, what is equally boring is when legitimate criticisms about an imperfect work are always rationalized away by silly logical fallacies, and nobody sees fit to properly draw attention to this.

[]: Post by J_Faulkner » Sat Apr 24, 2010 1:53 pm Merridian wrote: A work of art is "just" a work of art. The viewer cannot expect it to cater to his/her expectations, and as such, must meet it at least halfway. That is the whole purpose to critique and interpretation through discussion.

[]: Post by tomrule123 » Sat Apr 24, 2010 1:57 pm CyberXIII wrote: There's no such thing as a perfect anime. Everything has flaws, and through those flaws do we have better anime. Besides, if we had a perfect anime, no one would recognize it as such because anime fans will nitpick it to death.

[]: Post by ran1 » Sat Apr 24, 2010 1:59 pm We have some very powerful "art-fags" and Anno worshippers around here. Their numbers are great, and they are capable of operating as colonial organisms

[]: Post by NemZ » Sat Apr 24, 2010 2:00 pm Personally I think the unfinished style of 25/26 works remarkably well with the subject matter, so I don't see that as a flaw. In point of fact, the 'morphing symbols' (ending with Rei's eye) bit of 26 was one of my favorite shots of the entire series. I also think Jimbo has it somewhat right when he says that the show may have had a net benefit because of the budget crunch. It's well known that obstacles increase creativity while the other end, a blank slate, is incredibly stifling. NGE is definately flawed (ahem, action arc)... but significantly less flawed than many contemporary works.

Why are we, or, more importantly, why should we be obsessed with something's lack of perfection? This subject has come up a lot recentl

[]: Post by NemZ » Sat Apr 24, 2010 2:00 pm Personally I think the unfinished style of 25/26 works remarkably well with the subject matter, so I don't see that as a flaw. In point of fact, the 'morphing symbols' (ending with Rei's eye) bit of 26 was one of my favorite shots of the entire series. I also think Jimbo has it somewhat right when he says that the show may have had a net benefit because of the budget crunch. It's well known that obstacles increase creativity while the other end, a blank slate, is incredibly stifling. NGE is definately flawed (ahem, action arc)... but significantly less flawed than many contemporary works.
